name: Teutates
description: Teutates, also known as Toutatis, is a prominent Celtic deity venerated across Gaul and Britain. His name, derived from the Proto-Celtic *teuta- meaning "tribe" or "people," suggests his role as a protector of the community. Teutates is generally considered a tribal god, embodying the collective strength and well-being of the Celtic people. Roman writers often associated him with Mercury or Mars, reflecting his diverse attributes. While textual evidence is scarce, archaeological findings, including votive offerings and inscriptions, attest to his widespread worship. The infamous passage from Lucan's *Pharsalia* describing human sacrifice to Teutates by drowning victims in vats paints a grim picture, though the historicity and prevalence of this practice are debated among scholars. Nevertheless, Teutates remains a significant figure in Celtic religious beliefs, representing the power and unity of the tribe.
subtitle: Protector of the Tribe

keyMyths.title: The Cauldron of Plenty
keyMyths.description: Legend tells of a magical cauldron associated with Teutates, capable of providing endless sustenance to his followers. This cauldron, often depicted in Celtic art, symbolizes the abundance and prosperity that Teutates bestows upon those under his protection. The cauldron never runs dry, ensuring that the tribe has all it needs to thrive, reflecting Teutates' role as a provider and sustainer of his people. It is often said that only those loyal to the tribe and Teutates may partake of its bounty.
keyMyths.source: Green, Miranda. *The Gods of the Celts*. Barnes & Noble, 1993.

keyMyths.title: Teutates and the Sacred Oak
keyMyths.description: The oak tree holds a special significance in Celtic culture, and it is closely linked to Teutates. Considered a sacred tree, the oak represents strength, endurance, and wisdom. Rituals dedicated to Teutates often took place within oak groves, where priests would seek his guidance and blessings for the tribe. The Druids believed that Teutates resided within the ancient oaks, listening to the prayers and concerns of his people. The felling of an oak was considered a sacrilege, an offense against Teutates himself.
keyMyths.source: Ellis Davidson, H. R. *Gods and Myths of Northern Europe*. Penguin Books, 1964.

keyMyths.title: The Boar Hunt of Teutates
keyMyths.description: The boar, a symbol of strength and ferocity, is frequently associated with Teutates. Myth tells of a great boar hunt led by the god himself, a demonstration of his power and skill. The hunt represents the constant struggle against adversity and the need for the tribe to be strong and vigilant. Capturing the boar signifies the triumph of order over chaos and the protection of the community from external threats. The boar's meat, consumed after the hunt, symbolizes the sharing of Teutates' strength with the tribe.

sources.author: Lucan
